title: Last-minute Christmas cake
subtitle: A rich fruit cake that does not need weeks of feeding
excerpt: Most Christmas cake recipes insist on months of advance preparation. This one skips the long feed and soaks the dried fruit overnight instead, which gives a moist, boozy cake without the wait. It is still recognisably a traditional dark British fruit cake, just made to a shorter timescale.
sourceNotes: The traditional British Christmas cake has roots in medieval plum porridge and eighteenth-century plum cake, evolving into the dark, alcohol-rich, marzipan-topped form that became standard in Victorian times. The overnight soak as a substitute for weeks of feeding is a well-established adaptation found in British food writing from the 1990s onward. This recipe is synthesised from the established British Christmas cake tradition with the abbreviated method.

recipe.freezeNotes: The undecorated cake freezes well for up to 3 months, double-wrapped in cling film. Thaw overnight at room temperature before marzipanning and icing.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Soak the fruit the night before baking. The baked cake improves if left wrapped for at least 5 days before cutting, even without the long feeding schedule.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: conventional oven; a slow oven prevents the outside burning before the centre sets. Cover with a double layer of brown paper wrapped around the tin.

glossaryTerms.definition: Soaking dried fruit overnight in alcohol or orange juice plumps the fruit and distributes the booze evenly before baking, achieving a similar result to weeks of post-bake feeding. The fruit absorbs the liquid as it sits, so none evaporates in the oven. For a non-alcoholic version, strong brewed tea or orange juice works just as well as brandy or rum.

content.text: The shortcut in this recipe is the

. Instead of baking the cake weeks ahead and feeding it repeatedly with brandy, you soak the dried fruit the night before in enough alcohol to get it properly boozy. The result is a dense, dark, properly moist cake that tastes traditional without the waiting time.

Wrap the outside of the tin in a double layer of brown paper tied with string before baking. The paper insulates the sides and prevents the fruit on the outside from scorching during the long, slow bake.

Combine the 150 g raisins, 150 g sultanas, 100 g currants and 50 g mixed peel in a bowl. Pour over the 80 ml dark rum or brandy, stir well, cover and leave at room temperature overnight. All the alcohol should be absorbed by morning.

Heat the oven to 150 degrees C (conventional). Line a 20 cm round tin with a double layer of greased baking paper on the base and sides, the paper extending 5 cm above the rim. Wrap the outside of the tin with a double layer of brown paper or newspaper tied securely with string.

Beat the 175 g softened butter and 175 g dark brown sugar until creamy. Beat in the 3 eggs one at a time, then stir in the 1 tbsp black treacle. Fold in the 200 g flour, 2 tsp mixed spice, 1 tsp cinnamon, half a teaspoon baking powder, a pinch of salt and 50 g ground almonds. Stir in all the soaked fruit. Spoon into the tin and level the top. Bake for 2 hours 30 minutes until a skewer comes out clean. Leave in the tin until completely cold before wrapping and storing. Allow at least 5 days before cutting for the texture to settle.

items.problem: Outside burning before the centre is baked
items.solution: The oven is too hot, or the tin protection was inadequate. Check that the outside of the tin is wrapped in double paper and that the oven temperature is no higher than 150 degrees C. If the top is darkening, lay a sheet of baking paper loosely over it.

The batter was too soft when the fruit was folded in. Make sure the butter and sugar are properly creamed so the batter has some structure, and fold the fruit in firmly enough that it is well distributed rather than sitting in pools.
